items.passage: Evolution describes the gradual process through which populations of living organisms change across many successive generations. Individuals with helpful traits tend to survive longer and produce more offspring than their rivals. These advantageous traits are then passed to the next generation through inherited genetic material. Over time, the useful features become common while the weaker ones slowly disappear from the group. This steady natural process explains the remarkable variety of species found on Earth today.

Sustainable agriculture seeks methods that protect the soil while still producing enough food for growing populations. Farmers often rotate their crops so that the land can recover its natural nutrients. Cover plants are grown between seasons to shield the ground from wind and heavy rain. Many growers also reduce chemical sprays and rely on insects that hunt harmful pests. These careful practices help keep farmland healthy and productive for many future generations to come.

Attention is the mental ability that allows people to focus on certain information while ignoring distractions. The human brain simply cannot process every signal that reaches the senses at once. By selecting what matters most, a person can respond quickly to important events nearby. Loud noises or sudden movements can capture attention even when someone tries to concentrate. Psychologists study these natural limits to understand how memory and learning depend on steady focus.

The electric telegraph transformed long-distance communication by allowing coded messages to travel across entire continents almost instantly. Before its invention, news often took weeks to reach distant towns by horse or ship. Operators tapped out words using a simple code of short and long electrical signals. Wires were soon stretched between cities, and later cables were laid beneath the ocean. This rapid new network reshaped business, journalism, and government throughout the entire nineteenth century.

Fossils are the preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms that lived millions of years ago. Most form when a dead plant or animal is quickly buried under layers of sediment. Over long periods, minerals slowly replace the original tissue and harden into solid rock. Scientists carefully compare these clues to learn how life on Earth changed over time. Each fossil offers a rare window into environments that vanished long before humans existed.

Mars exploration has fascinated scientists who hope to understand whether the planet once supported living things. Robotic rovers have traveled across the dusty surface, collecting samples of soil and rock. Their powerful cameras and instruments send valuable data back to eager researchers waiting on Earth. Evidence suggests that liquid water once flowed through ancient valleys and dry riverbeds. Future crewed missions may finally carry human astronauts to explore this cold and distant world.

Cellular respiration is the chemical process by which living cells release the energy stored inside food molecules. This reaction usually needs oxygen, which the blood delivers to every part of the body. Sugar from digested meals is gradually broken down to power the body's vital daily activities. The busy cells then produce carbon dioxide and water as ordinary leftover waste products. Without this constant supply of energy, muscles and organs could not function properly.

A lens is a curved piece of glass that bends light to form clearer, sharper images. When rays pass through the surface, they change direction and meet at a single point. Convex lenses bulge outward and can gather sunlight into a small, bright spot. Such lenses appear in cameras, microscopes, and the eyeglasses that many people wear daily. By controlling how light travels, these tools help humans see the very small and distant.

Rock art includes the ancient paintings and carvings that early peoples created on cave and cliff surfaces. Many images show animals such as deer, bison, and horses that were hunted for food. Artists ground colored minerals into powder and mixed them with fat or water. These striking pictures were often placed deep inside dark caves, far from any natural daylight. Researchers now believe such art recorded the beliefs, stories, and events important to each community.

The ocean twilight zone is a dim, mysterious layer where only faint sunlight manages to reach. It lies far below the bright surface, between roughly two hundred and one thousand meters. Many strange creatures here produce their own light to attract prey or confuse hunters. Every night, countless small animals rise upward to feed under the cover of darkness. Scientists study this hidden zone to learn how it stores carbon from the atmosphere.

Self-healing materials are remarkable substances specially designed to repair their own damage without any human help. Some contain tiny capsules of liquid that burst open whenever a crack begins to spread. The escaping liquid quickly fills the gap and hardens to restore the original strength. Engineers hope these clever materials will make bridges, roads, and phones last much longer. Such repairs could reduce waste and save enormous amounts of money over time.

Radar is a technology that uses radio waves to detect distant objects and measure their distance. A powerful transmitter sends out invisible waves that spread rapidly through the open air. When these traveling waves strike a solid object, part of the signal bounces straight back. The returning echo reveals how far away the target sits and how fast it moves. Radar now guides airplanes, tracks storms, and helps ships travel safely at night.

Hail consists of solid, rounded balls of ice that form inside the tall, powerful thunderstorm clouds. Strong upward winds carry cold raindrops high into the freezing upper regions of the cloud. There the drops turn to ice and gather additional layers with each trip upward. Once the frozen lumps grow far too heavy, they finally fall rapidly toward the ground. Large hailstones can damage crops, dent cars, and break windows during severe storms.

Carnivorous plants are unusual species that capture insects to gain nutrients missing from poor, thin soil. Most of them grow in wet bogs where the ground offers very little natural nourishment. The famous Venus flytrap snaps its hinged leaves shut the moment a visitor touches them. Other kinds trap victims in sticky fluid or drown them inside deep, slippery tubes. Special juices then dissolve the prey so the plant can absorb the useful minerals.

An ant colony functions as a single, organized community in which thousands of insects cooperate closely. A large queen lays all the eggs, while workers gather food and defend the nest. The tiny workers communicate by releasing chemical scents along their busy travel routes. These invisible trails guide others toward fresh food or warn them about nearby danger. Through such teamwork, a colony can survive challenges that no single ant could face.

Banking began thousands of years ago when merchants needed a safe place to store valuable goods. Early temples often guarded stored grain and precious metals for wealthy traders and ordinary farmers. Over time, keepers began lending part of these deposits to people who needed money. Borrowers repaid the original loan later, usually adding a small extra charge called interest. These simple practices slowly grew into the vast banking systems we depend on today.

Sign languages are complete, natural human languages that rely on hand shapes, movement, and facial expression. They are not simply gestures, but systems with their own grammar and rich vocabulary. Deaf communities all around the world have developed many different signed languages over the centuries. A single handshape can change its meaning depending on where it appears in space. Young children exposed to signing learn it just as naturally as others learn spoken words.

Linear perspective is a drawing technique that makes a flat painted picture appear to have real depth. Artists during the Italian Renaissance discovered clever mathematical rules for showing distance on a flat canvas. Lines that move away from the viewer seem to meet at a single point. Objects placed nearer the front are drawn larger than those far in the background. This powerful method allowed painters to create scenes that looked strikingly lifelike and truly believable.

A social network describes the complex web of relationships that connects individuals within a larger community. Each person is closely linked to friends, relatives, and coworkers through countless everyday social ties. Information, habits, and even strong emotions can travel quickly along these hidden personal connections. Researchers carefully map such networks to see how new ideas and behaviors spread among people. A single well-connected individual can influence the opinions of an entire surrounding group.

A binary star system contains two separate stars that orbit each other around a shared center. Such pairs are surprisingly common, and many stars in the sky belong to them. Their steady, predictable motion lets astronomers precisely calculate the mass of each glowing partner. Sometimes one star slowly pulls hot glowing gas away from its close neighbor across space. Studying these systems helps scientists understand how stars are born, live, and eventually die.
